title: The Password Loop
blurb: Third lockout this week. He is NOT happy about it.
hardBlurb: dale again. hes furious. says he has a client mtg at the top of the hour.

n1.text:
The queue note reads: "Dale — locked out AGAIN. Third time this week."

He answers on the first ring: "Let me guess. You're going to reset it and tell me to be more careful. THIRD TIME. And I have a client meeting in twenty minutes."
n1.hint: Three identical tickets in one week is a pattern, not a coincidence — and he's angrier at the pattern than at the password.

choices.label: "You're right — three lockouts in a week isn't normal, and it's not on you. Let's get you into that meeting first, then I want to find what's actually causing this."
choices.feedback: Acknowledge the pattern before touching the keyboard. Dale isn't angry at the password — he's angry that nobody has treated ticket #3 any differently than ticket #1.

choices.label: "No problem, resetting it right now."
choices.feedback: Fast, polite… and exactly what happened the last two times. If you change nothing about the call, you've just scheduled the fourth one.

choices.label: "Have you been typing it with caps lock on?"
choices.feedback: You just told a frustrated repeat caller that he's the problem — before checking a single log. Even when user error IS the cause, leading with the accusation guarantees a fight.

n2.text: "Fine. Just reset it." You pull up his account. Locked, as advertised.
n2.hint: Anyone can claim to be Dale. What kind of proof doesn't depend on the caller's phone, voice, or mailbox?

choices.label: "Before I reset anything I need to verify you — I'm going to call you right back on the number we have on file for Kettler & Boone."
choices.feedback: "Reset my password" is the oldest social-engineering move in the book. Verification isn't distrust of Dale — it's protection FOR Dale, and skipping it because the caller is annoyed is exactly what an attacker counts on.

choices.label: He sounds like Dale and it's his extension calling — good enough, reset it.
choices.feedback: Caller ID is spoofable and voices can be cloned. If this account ever gets breached, "he sounded right" in the ticket notes is a resume-updating event.

choices.label: "Email me from your work account to confirm it's you."
choices.feedback: He's locked out of that account — and an attacker who already owns the mailbox passes this check instantly. Verification has to be out-of-band: a callback to the number on file.

n3.text:
Verified. You reset the password and he's back in with five minutes to spare.

"Great. Bye." You could close the ticket right now and nobody would blink.
n3.hint: The reset fixes the symptom. What question would find the cause of lockout #4 before it happens?

choices.label: "One more minute, Dale — when does it usually lock you out? First thing in the morning? After you've been out of the office?"
choices.feedback: Three lockouts is a pattern, and patterns have root causes. The questions cost sixty seconds; ticket #4 costs an hour of everyone's time and the last of Dale's patience.

choices.label: Close the ticket — he got what he asked for.
choices.feedback: He got what he asked for, not what he needed. Ticket #4 is now booked for Thursday, and it opens with "FOURTH TIME."

choices.label: Set his account so the password never expires — that'll stop it.
choices.feedback: That weakens security policy to silence a symptom — and it wouldn't even work. Expiry isn't what locks an account; failed logon attempts are. Something is still trying a bad password.

n4.text:
"…Now that you mention it, it always seems to happen after I've been out at client sites."

There it is. His phone's mail app still has the OLD password saved — it's been retrying every few minutes, and five failures trips the lockout policy. Every time he leaves the building, his own pocket locks him out.
n4.hint: The account isn't locking itself. Something Dale carries still knows the old password.

choices.label: Walk him through updating the saved password on his phone, and stay on the line until mail syncs clean.
choices.feedback: Root cause found AND fixed in one call. Stale cached credentials — phones, mapped drives, saved sessions — cause a huge share of "mystery" lockouts. Always ask: what else knows the old password?

choices.label: Tell him to keep his phone off the office Wi-Fi.
choices.feedback: The mail app retries over cellular too. This "fix" just makes the lockouts look random instead of predictable — which is worse, because now nobody will ever find it.

choices.label: Explain the cause and let him update the phone himself after his meeting.
choices.feedback: "Later" means the old password fails again at 5pm and he starts tomorrow locked out. You are one password field away from done — finish it while you have him.

n5.text:
Phone updated, mail syncing, account clean.

Dale, in a completely different tone: "Huh. Nobody ever explained WHY it kept happening. Thanks."
n5.hint: The ticket is done — but the relationship, and the fix that prevents the whole class of problem, are still on the table.

choices.label: "Glad we found it. One thought — self-service reset and MFA would let your whole office fix this kind of thing in seconds. Mind if I pass your ticket to our account manager as the example?"
choices.feedback: A closed loop plus a proactive suggestion turns your worst repeat caller into the guy who tells his office "IT finally fixed it." Real tickets like this are exactly how good MSPs sell the right projects.

choices.label: "No problem. Have a good meeting!"
choices.feedback: A perfectly pleasant close — but you had a warm lead on a real improvement (SSPR/MFA) and let it walk. Tickets teach you things the account manager needs to know.

choices.label: "Just be careful where you save your passwords from now on."
choices.feedback: You just undid the entire call. He didn't "save it wrong" — a stale credential hammered the lockout policy. Don't hand the blame back to the client on your way out the door.

n6.text:
Ticket notes: root cause — stale cached credential in mobile mail app. Fixed on call. Identity verified via callback. SSPR/MFA recommendation sent to account manager with client buy-in.

Dale's follow-up email to your boss: "Give this guy a raise."

Ticket closed.
